topic: European news from the last week on deletion of political social media posts, controversial online speech, online speech removal, censorship, moderation disputes, and court or regulatory takedown orders

articles.headline_en: Could a UK Teen Social Media Ban Work Without Expanding Surveillance
articles.summary_en: The article says the UK government has confirmed a ban on social media for under-16s, with legislation expected before the end of the year and possible entry into force in spring 2027. It also reports that the government plans to require hardware manufacturers to install or activate software to detect and block explicit images, with enforcement backed by possible fines and criminal liability if companies do not comply.
articles.significance: This matters because it shows a major European government moving toward stronger control over online speech and access, while raising concerns about surveillance and age verification.

articles.headline_en: Gen Z Labour MP Says Social Media Ban Will "Create More Problems" for Young People
articles.summary_en: The item reports that Labour MP Josh Dean said the UK government's ban on social media for under-16s will 'create more problems' for young people. It frames the measure as politically contentious even within the governing party.
articles.significance: This matters because it captures internal political criticism of a major online speech restriction in the UK.

editorial_overview: The available results are dominated by the UK government's planned under-16 social media ban and the related debate over enforcement, surveillance, and age verification. They show a clear policy trend toward tighter regulation of online access rather than examples of deleted posts, censorship orders, or moderation disputes involving specific political figures. No strong Europe-based examples of politicians deleting posts or courts ordering the removal of political speech appeared in the provided results.
